coredata.dc:title: Construction of nanocomposite hydrogel by TiO2-carbon quantum dots encapsulated in alginate with a highly efficient adsorption and photodegradation of dye pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Taghiloo, Bita
Shahnazi, Azita
Nabid, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: This presentation introduces an integrated photocatalyst/adsorbent system, achieved through immobilization and encapsulation of the TiO2 doped with CQDs (carbon quantum dots) nanocomposite within the calcium alginate (CaAlg) matrix. The nanoparticles and nanocomposites were analyzed using various techniques, including FE-SEM, EDX, TEM, XRD, PL, FT-IR, BET, and UV–vis DRS, to determine their structure and properties. The TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel considerably inﬂuenced the adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) as a dye pollutant. The adsorption studies were conducted to assess parameters such as adsorption capacity, kinetic behavior, and adsorption isotherms. The outcomes revealed a high adsorption capacity (44.13 mg/g at 90 min) and indicated that the pseudo-second-order kinetic model was highly suitable for describing the adsorption behavior of MB. Moreover, the Freundlich isotherm exhibited better fitting capabilities compared to the Langmuir isotherm. CQDs not only facilitated the transfer of photoelectrons from the TiO2 surface, preventing recombination of electron-hole pairs but also enhanced visible light absorption by the upconversion photoluminescence. The TiO2/CQDs/Alg displayed excellent photodegradation performance (97 %) of MB compared with TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs under visible light due to the abundance of active sites for MB adsorption and photocatalytic reactions. The TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel exhibited excellent reusability in degrading MB for up to five consecutive cycles and showed significant swelling behavior across a broad pH range, reflecting the hydrogel's exceptional stability. The use of charge carrier scavengers indicated that O2˙−radicals were the primary surface species involved in the photodegradation of MB.

head.ce:title: Construction of nanocomposite hydrogel by TiO2-carbon quantum dots encapsulated in alginate with a highly efficient adsorption and photodegradation of dye pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer and Materials Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, GC, Tehran 1983969411, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer and Materials Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, GC

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer and Materials Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, GC, 1983969411 Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This presentation introduces an integrated photocatalyst/adsorbent system, achieved through immobilization and encapsulation of the TiO2 doped with CQDs (carbon quantum dots) nanocomposite within the calcium alginate (CaAlg) matrix. The nanoparticles and nanocomposites were analyzed using various techniques, including FE-SEM, EDX, TEM, XRD, PL, FT-IR, BET, and UV–vis DRS, to determine their structure and properties. The TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel considerably inﬂuenced the adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) as a dye pollutant. The adsorption studies were conducted to assess parameters such as adsorption capacity, kinetic behavior, and adsorption isotherms. The outcomes revealed a high adsorption capacity (44.13 mg/g at 90 min) and indicated that the pseudo-second-order kinetic model was highly suitable for describing the adsorption behavior of MB. Moreover, the Freundlich isotherm exhibited better fitting capabilities compared to the Langmuir isotherm. CQDs not only facilitated the transfer of photoelectrons from the TiO2 surface, preventing recombination of electron-hole pairs but also enhanced visible light absorption by the upconversion photoluminescence. The TiO2/CQDs/Alg displayed excellent photodegradation performance (97 %) of MB compared with TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs under visible light due to the abundance of active sites for MB adsorption and photocatalytic reactions. The TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel exhibited excellent reusability in degrading MB for up to five consecutive cycles and showed significant swelling behavior across a broad pH range, reflecting the hydrogel's exceptional stability. The use of charge carrier scavengers indicated that O2˙−radicals were the primary surface species involved in the photodegradation of MB.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid population growth and shortage of drinking water due to droughts, uncontrolled extraction of groundwater, and pollution are two major challenges facing the world that are rapidly increasing. These two phenomena are considered a serious threat to the future of humanity [1]. Furthermore, with technological advancements, pollution of rivers, lakes, and groundwater owing to the existence of chemical compounds such as heavy metals, pesticides and insecticides, hydrocarbons, nutrients, dyes, and pharmaceuticals is another serious problem worldwide [2]. Azo dyes are synthetic dyes with a wide range of applications in various industries, including textiles, leather, and paper. Many azo dyes are dangerous to the environment and human health due to their persistence, toxicity, and potential for bioaccumulation [3]. Therefore, removing azo dyes from wastewater before discharging into the environment is very important. For this purpose, various methods have been developed, including physical, chemical, and biological treatments [4]. Adsorption using activated carbon or clay minerals, coagulation/flocculation with metal salts, and advanced oxidation processes such as electrolysis, ozonation, and photocatalysis are some effective techniques for removing azo dyes from industrial wastewater [5–10].

Over the past three decades, photocatalytic reactions have garnered significant scientific and technological attention. The primary reason for this extensive research is the unique properties of light-sensitive materials, which facilitate the efficient direct conversion of light into highly reactive chemical species. These characteristics are being utilized to address several pressing global issues, including pollutant degradation, photo-organic synthesis [11], and the direct splitting of water into hydrogen and oxygen [12,13]. Research on photocatalytic pollutant degradation is progressively expanding due to its significant industrial applications. The primary objective of using photocatalysts is to break down carcinogenic and harmful organic compounds found in industrial effluents while maintaining stability under light exposure [14]. Commonly utilized semiconductors, including metal oxides (such as TiO₂, ZnO, WO₃, CeO₂, NiFeOx, Hg₂WO₄), metal sulfides (like CdS, ZnS, Bi₂S₃, and WS₃), and even metal nitrides (such as Ta₃N₅ and Si₃N₄), offer an efficient platform for photocatalysis [12,13,15]. These photocatalysts can create electron-hole pairs when exposed to light. This process can generate reaction species like hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radicals, or superoxide ions by reacting with the adsorbed oxidants/reducers (O2 and OH-) [5,16]. These species can either oxidize or reduce organic pollutants, resulting in their complete mineralization into water, carbon dioxide, and inorganic substances. Photocatalysis boasts numerous benefits, such as high efficiency, low toxicity, reduced energy consumption, lowered operational costs, and minimal production of secondary pollutants [17,18].

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a well-known photocatalyst amongst metal oxides due to its superb performance, low cost, chemical stability, non-toxicity, and availability. However, limitations such as lack of activity in the visible spectral region, irregular distribution in solution, and high electron-hole recombination rate have hindered the widespread use of these nanoparticles in photocatalysis. To surmount these limitations, researchers have devised various strategies to improve the performance of TiO2 nanoparticles as photocatalysts [19–21].

One of these approaches involves coupling TiO2 nanoparticles with carbon-based materials like carbon nanotubes, graphene derivatives and carbon quantum dots. These nanocomposites can enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 by increasing visible light absorption, improving stability, and reducing the rate of electron-hole recombination [22,23].

Compared to other carbon nanomaterials, CQDs are unique due to the presence of oxygen-containing groups on their surface. These groups make CQDs highly soluble in aqueous solutions and allow for additional functionalization [24–26]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles that are nearly spherical or spherical with a diameter of less than 10 nm and are also known as fluorescent nanomaterials. In comparison with conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), CQDs have many advantages such as high stability, good solubility in water, excellent optical properties, easy surface modification, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [23,27]. Extensive research has been carried out on nanocomposites based on CQDs and their synthesis with nanocrystalline cores such as TiO2, g-C3N4, Bi2S3, and CdS as photocatalysts. These nanocomposites exhibit unique properties owing to the synergistic effects arising from the surface attachment between CQDs and nanocrystalline cores, broad visible light absorption, abundant reaction sites, and reduction in the recombination rate of charges [28,29].

However, these nanocomposites have a tendency to aggregate due to the instability of the nano-sized particles and the difficulty in achieving uniform distribution. This can potentially reduce their surface area, hinder their ability to absorb light, and ultimately decrease their photocatalytic efficiency. Additionally, there is the added challenge of separating and recovering the nanocomposites after treatment [30]. In order to address these limitations that impede large-scale application, a potentially effective solution would be to incorporate a new material into the system to serve as a support by embedding the nano-sized photocatalyst within it [31].

Moreover, photocatalytic efficiency can be enhanced by using the support matrix to concentrate pollutants. A versatile treatment strategy that incorporates both photocatalytic degradation and effective adsorption functions is more desirable. The hydrogel, as an ideal candidate for catalyst support and dye adsorption, has a three-dimensional network structure that allows for the concentration and adsorption of pollutants [32].

Alginate (Alg) is a natural, anionic, and non-toxic polysaccharide with hydrogel properties owing to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Alg can interact with semiconductor catalysts through functional groups, facilitating the separation of e--h+ pairs. Consequently, it is considered a suitable support for easily forming eco-friendly gels in water [33]. The use of Alg support effectively prevents the agglomeration of photocatalysts powder and improves stability [34]. Moreover, the interaction of functional groups as active sites with pollutants increases the adsorption capacity of pollutants on the Alg surface, thereby enhancing photocatalytic efficiency [32]. Both sodium and calcium alginate have been employed to synthesize photocatalytic composites with TiO2 nanoparticles for degrading organic pollutants in wastewater [35,36]. Alginate can form granules by substituting Na+ ions with polyvalent Ca2+ cations, resulting in cross-linking interactions. These interactions significantly enhance the binding efficiency of photocatalyst molecules [37].

Several studies have investigated the synthesis and performance of different alginate-based composites for various photocatalytic applications. Nawaz et al. incorporated reduced graphene oxide (rGO) into sodium alginate to enhance bonding with TiO2 and created a three-dimensional sodium alginate aerogel. The aerogel composite achieved a high degradation efficiency (over 99 %) for ibuprofen and sulfamethoxazole pollutants under UV-A light [37]. In another study, Nouri et al. used alginate calcium to prepare TiO2/CaAlg photobiocomposite beads via the extrusion method for adsorption/photocatalytic degradation of BB 41. The image of TiO₂/CaAlg beads showed a somewhat spherical shape with an irregular and rough surface, facilitating the adsorption of more BB 41 molecules. The dispersion of TiO2 within the CaAlg matrix led to a reduction in the TiO2/CaAlg band gap and an increase in the photodegradation efficiency of BB 41 dye [38]. Moreover, a floating alginate photocatalyst was synthesized with immobilized TiO2 for photocatalytic behavior. The nanoparticles had a rough surface and porous morphology and were capable of removing 100 % of the 2-naphthol pollutant [39]. Researchers developed a buoyant photocatalyst by modifying TiO2 immobilized in alginate polymer, achieving 100 % floating beads. Characterizations confirmed uniform beads with porous structure, exhibiting efficient tartrazine dye removal (up to 89 %) and sustained TOC removal over seven cycles under UV irradiation [40].

In this study, we introduced a novel approach to combine TiO₂, CQDs, and alginate to achieve effective adsorption and photodegradation of methylene blue as an azo dye under visible light. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, the modification of TiO₂/CQDs nanocomposite with alginate polymer layer has not been reported yet. Loading CQDs onto TiO2 surfaces enhanced the photo-response range, leading to greater photon absorption and improved efficiency in harvesting visible light. Alginate was utilized as a support material to encapsulate the TiO₂/CQDs nanocomposite, creating a porous structure that allowed for the selective adsorption of cationic azo dye by interacting with the alginate's negative carboxylate groups. Additionally, it stabilized the TiO₂/CQDs nanocomposite, making it easier to recover. Notably, the bio-nanocomposite hydrogel exhibited a great adsorption capacity and a high photodegradation efficiency for removal of methylene blue dye from aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. The ternary nanocomposite hydrogel was characterized using various analytical techniques, including XRD, FTIR, BET, DRS, FE-SEM, EDX, and TEM.

Titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4), Ammonium sulfate (NH₄) ₂SO₄), Ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) 25–30 %, Citric acid (CA) (C₆H₈O₇), polyethyleneimine (PEI), Sodium alginate (NaAlg) (C6H7NaO6)n, Methylene blue (MB) (C16H18ClN3S) dye, Calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2.2 H2O), p-Benzoquinone (C6H4O2), Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid ([CH2N(CH2CO2H)2]2), Methanol (CH3OH), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and Hydrochloric acid (HCl) were obtained from Merck Co., Ltd (Darmstadt, Germany). All components were of analytical or higher grade and used as received.

Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) and Energy-Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analyses were conducted utilizing the MIRA3 TESCAN FE-SEM device. The crystalline structure of the samples was explored employing a STOE XD-3A X-ray diffractometer, utilizing Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54056 Å) and operating at 40 kV and 40 mA. UV–vis characterization was performed using a UV-S600 spectrophotometer from Analytik Jena. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analyses were carried out with a Philips CM300 at 200 KV. Diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectra were recorded by an Ava Spec-2048TEC spectrophotometer. Further structural insights were obtained through Fourier-Transform Infrared (FT-IR) analyses conducted with a BOMEM MB-series FT-IR spectrometer using KBr pellets. The textural properties of the samples were determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) adsorption-desorption of N2 at −196 °C, facilitated by a Belsorp mini II instrument with a degassing temperature of 120 °C for 12 hours. Fluorescence spectra were acquired using an LS45 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer) equipped with a xenon lamp as the light source. Illumination of the samples was achieved using 168 Bridgelux LED lamps (3.2 V, 1 W, 350 mA, λ > 390 nm) in a circular arrangement.

To prepare TiO2 nanoparticles, 50 mL of a 1.5 M ammonium sulfate aqueous solution was mixed with a 0.75 M titanium tetrachloride solution and stirred for 5 minutes [41]. The resulting solution was subjected to reflux at 75 °C for 90 minutes. Next, a 2.5 M ammonium hydroxide solution was added drop by drop under high-speed stirring until a neutral pH was reached. The precipitated titanium hydroxide was then filtered, washed with water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C. TiO2 nanoparticles were obtained by calcining the dried material in air at 350 °C for 4 hours and subsequently cooled down to room temperature.

CQDs were synthesized using citric acid, PEI, and water in a Teflon-lined autoclave [42]. First, 0.7 mmol (0.1 g) of citric acid was mixed with a 0.2 g of PEA in 10 mL of deionized (DI) water, followed by magnetic stirring to form a uniform solution for 20 min. The resulting solution was then placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 4 hours. After the reaction time, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the solution was ﬁltered through a Whatman ﬁlter paper to remove the large particles. The remaining particles were eliminated by centrifuging at 7000 rpm for 15 min, resulting in the collection of the supernatant. The final product was dried at 30°C under vacuum for 24 h. The synthesized carbon quantum dots were designated as CQDs.

The TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite was prepared through a straightforward and convenient process conducted at room temperature [43]. To achieve high performance of CQDs in the nanocomposite, two TiO2/CQDs nanocomposites with different weight percentages of CQDs (1 % and 5 %) were prepared. Initially, 0.5 g of TiO2 nanoparticles were dispersed in 2.4 mL of DI water. Subsequently, the dispersed TiO2 nanoparticles were added to different amounts of a CQDs aqueous solution with a concentration of 1 mg/mL and mixed at high speed for one hour. The resulting nanocomposites were dried at 65°C under vacuum for 90 min and were labeled as TiO2/CQDs 1 % and TiO2/CQDs 5 %.

Initially, a 3 % w/v solution of sodium alginate was prepared by adding 3 g of sodium alginate to 100 mL of deionized (DI) water and stirring for 3 h [44]. For the second solution, 1 g of a synthesized TiO2/CQDs 5 % nanocomposite was dispersed in 100 mL of DI water using sonication for 15 min. Then, 3 g of calcium chloride dihydrate was added to the solution and stirred. Simultaneously, the sodium alginate solution was gradually added dropwise to the second solution. The resulting mixture was gently stirred for 12 hours. Afterward, excess Ca2+ ions and TiO2 were removed by washing multiple times with DI water, and the product was stored in DI water. The synthesized nanocomposite hydrogel was named TiO2/CQDs/Alg. For any characterization purpose, freeze-drying of hydrogel was conducted. Fig. 1 depicts a schematic representation detailing the synthesis process employed for the fabrication of TiO2/CQDs/Alg.

The photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel was evaluated by degradation of a cationic dye, methylene blue (MB), under visible light illumination using LED lamps (λ >390 nm) in ambient condition. For this purpose, 8 mg of the hydrogel photocatalyst was suspended in 20 mL of a 5 ppm MB aqueous solution. The mixture was placed in the dark for 15 min on a magnetic stirrer to attain an adsorption–desorption equilibrium of the dye on the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel surface. The mixture was then exposed to light radiation while constantly stirring. The final concentration of MB was determined using the UV−vis spectrophotometer at λ = 655 nm after centrifuging of 3 mL of the mixture and removing the photocatalyst. Furthermore, TiO2 nanoparticles and a TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite were employed as controls to assess and compare their photocatalytic efficiency with that of TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel. For a more accurate comparison, the amounts of these photocatalysts were adjusted based on their weight percentages in the hydrogel.

The photocatalytic degradation efficiency was calculated utilizing the following [45]:

In the investigation of the adsorption process of hydrogel photocatalyst, 8 mg of TiO2/CQDs/Alg photocatalyst was added to a 20 mL of MB aqueous solution with initial concentrations of 5, 10, 15, 20, and 30 mg/L, and stirred for 90 min. At regular intervals of 15 min, a 3 mL aliquot of the mixture was taken out and centrifuged. Following the removal of the photocatalyst, the concentration of the MB was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at λ = 655 nm. The adsorption capacity (Q) of the photocatalyst was determined by the following equation [46]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of zero point of charge (pHzpc) of TiO2/CQDs/Alg
ce:section.ce:para: The point at which an adsorbent's surface has a neutral charge is known as the zero point of charge (pHzpc). Determining the pHzpc involves a pH titration process. In this experiment, 20 mL of 0.01 M NaCl solution was added to Erlenmeyer flasks with 8 mg of TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel. The initial pH of each flask was adjusted to a range of 2–11 using 0.1 M solutions of NaOH and HCl. The hydrogel solutions were shaken for 24 h at ambient temperature (100 rpm), and their final pH were measured. The pHzpc was identified as the point where the plot of ΔpH (pHinitial-pHfinal) against initial pH crossed the line initial pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the solution pH on TiO2/CQDs/Alg stability
ce:section.ce:para:
The stability of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel was studied by investigating the impact of pH on its swelling. Various tests were conducted across a wide pH range (2–12) to encompass all potential variations. The hydrogels were initially weighed (w0) after removing excess surface water and then immersed in solutions of differing pH at 25°C. After 24 h, the hydrogels were removed, and their final weight (wf) was measured after blotting away the surface solution with a piece of paper [47]. The swelling ratio (SR) was then calculated using the following equation [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The UV–vis and PL of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the aqueous solution of CQDs (1 mg/L) were studied. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs solution is depicted in Fig. 2a. The absorption peaks observed at 229 nm and 360 nm can be attributed to distinct molecular transitions. CQDs exhibit enhanced photon harvesting capabilities, particularly in the short-wavelength range. This heightened efficiency is attributed to the absorption of light facilitated by π-conjugated electrons within the sp2 atomic framework of the CQDs. Hence, the peak at 229 nm is associated with the π→π* transition of aromatic CC units, indicating the involvement of conjugated double bonds [49]. The peak at 360 nm corresponds to the n→π* transitions of the CO and C–N bonding groups [42,50].

The fluorescence spectra of CQDs under various excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 2b. The emission reaches its maximum peak at 510 nm, corresponding to an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. This strong visible-light emission of CQDs is associated with its quantum-sized graphite structure [51]. Generally, CQDs exhibit fluorescence through two mechanisms: bandgap transitions in conjugated π-domains and surface defects. Conjugated π-domains localize electron-hole pairs, while the surface acts as energy traps. Fluorescence results from electron-hole pair recombination in sp2 domains. In addition, the localized π and π* levels of the sp2 domains, situated between the s and s* states bandgaps of the sp3 matrix, contribute to photoluminescence. Functionalization and surface passivation enhance defect stability, enabling easier recombination of electrons and holes on the surface and intensifying emission [52,53].

The crystal structures of TiO2 nanoparticles, CQDs, and the TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite were analyzed through XRD, and the outcomes are represented in Fig. 3 and Table S1. In the XRD pattern of TiO2, the tetragonal anatase phase is confirmed, aligning with JCPDS standard card no. 21–1272. Characteristic diffraction peaks at 2Ө = 25.3°, 37.84°, 48.04°, 53.9°, 55°, 62.8°, 71.6°, and 75° correspond to the planes (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (220), and (215), respectively [38]. The XRD pattern of CQDs reveals characteristics peaks of graphitic (sp2) carbon ((JCPDS 26–1076) at 2Ө = 16.3°, 19.2°, 20.19°, 24.4°. These peaks are attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms, similar to the spacing found in a graphite lattice [54,55]. Additionally, certain impurity peaks arise from incomplete carbonization and residual groups from organic materials that remain on the surface of the CQDs [56]. The XRD spectrum for the TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite indicates no signals related to CQDs, which can be attributed to their small quantities (5 % of nanocomposite), high dispersion, and low crystallinity in the TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite [57].

FT-IR analysis was employed to characterize the functional groups of the synthesized nanoparticles and nanocomposites. In the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs ( Fig. 4a), the existence of -COOH functionality derived from citric acid on the surface of CQDs is validated by the broad band in the region of 2700–3300 cm−1, corresponding to O-H stretching vibration. The peak at 3423 cm−1 proves the existence of amino functional groups. Additionally, the bands at 2927 cm−1,1710 cm−1, and 1690 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of C-H, CO, and amide (C(=O)(–NH)), respectively. Furthermore, the peaks at 1390 cm−1 and 1303 cm−1 signify the presence of C-O and C-N stretching, respectively. The bending vibration of N-H appears at 1550 cm−1 [42].

In the TiO2 nanoparticle spectrum (Fig. 4b), the peak at 3210 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of O-H, while the peak at 1640 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibration of O-H (Ti-O-H). Moreover, the peak at 670 cm−1 is attributed to the Ti-O stretching band, representing the characteristic peak of TiO2 [58].

In the FT-IR spectrum of TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite (Fig. 4c), the major diﬀerences observed compared to the TiO2 nanoparticles’ spectrum. The enhanced presence of the O-H vibrational band at approximately 3200 (quite similar to O-H stretching vibration of TiO2) is related to the strong hydrogen bonding interaction between the oxygenated hydrophilic functional groups at the surface of CQDs and TiO2. The peak at 1640 cm−1 of O-H bending vibration for TiO2 has shifted to 1630 cm−1 for TiO2/CQDs. Besides, the new peaks at 1700 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 are attributed to CO and amide (C(=O)(–NH)) groups of CQDs which compared to those of in CQDs’spectrum have shifted to lower wavenumbers. Moreover, C-O stretching vibration of CQDs has emerged in higher wavenumber (at 1450 cm−1) in the spectrum of TiO2/CQDs. A new peak at 1370 cm−1 is also observed, representing the epoxy C–O stretching vibration in TiO2/CQDs [59]. These shifts and the emergence of a new C–O peak can be attributed to successful interactions between TiO2 and CQDs. Finally, the broad absorption band centered at 800 cm−1 is compared to that of the uncoated TiO2 demonstrating an eﬀective interaction in TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite through the combination of Ti-O-C and Ti-O-Ti vibrational bands [60].

The encapsulation of TiO2/CQDs within alginate is confirmed by the main characteristic absorption bands of the TiO2/CQDs and the emergence of new bands related to the Alg polymer (Fig. 4d). The main O-H stretching peak at 3200 cm−1 in the TiO2/CQDs spectrum has intensified in the TiO2/CQDs/Alg spectrum and shifted to a higher wavenumber of 3230 cm−1. This shift is attributed to the hydrogen bonding interactions between the hydroxyl groups on the surface of TiO2/CQDs and those in the alginate molecules. C-H stretching vibration originated from CQDs and alginate appears at 2925 cm−1. Furthermore, the stretching vibrations of carbonyl, amid, and epoxy groups of TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite are detected at 1700 cm−1, 1644 cm−1, and 1363 cm−1. The peaks at 1583 cm−1 and 1411 cm−1 signify the asymmetric and symmetric CO vibrations of alginate carboxyl groups. This observation confirms a specific type of bonding between COO- and Ca2+ in calcium alginate. In addition, the strong peak at 1022 cm−1 is attributed to C-C and C-O-C vibrations of the glycosidic bonds of alginate. The weak peaks at 1294 cm−1 and 875 cm−1 are assigned to C-H deformation and are more speciﬁc of M blocks of alginate [38]. Finally, some peaks around 650–750 cm−1 can be related to Ti–O stretching vibration and Ti–O–Ti bridging stretching mode. This suggests the presence of TiO6 octahedra and the formation of calcium titanate, indicating an effective interaction between CaAlg and TiO2/CQDs [61]. The FT-IR peak assignments of CQDs, TiO2, TiO2/CQDs, and TiO2/CQDs/Alg are shown in Table S2.

FE-SEM images were captured for TiO2 nanoparticles, alginate polymer, and the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel, as illustrated in Fig. 5. The nanoparticle structure of TiO2 is depicted in Fig. 5a, revealing spherical particles with a size range of 15–20 nm and a uniform dispersion pattern. Fig. 5b showcases the alginate polymer image, demonstrating a well-connected and continuous pore structure [62]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the encapsulation of the TiO2/CQDs nanocomposites by the alginate polymer is evident in the reduction of the size of Alg pores, coupled with a denser texture compared to that of Alg alone. The mapping of Ti in Fig. 5d provides further insight, illustrating that the spherical TiO2/CQDs nanocomposites are embedded within the alginate polymer tissue, thereby confirming the successful synthesis of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel. Notably, a discernible layer of alginate is observed covering the surface of TiO2/CQDs, indicative of a cohesive integration in the nanocomposite hydrogel structure.

The EDX spectra of the Alg polymer and the TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel are presented in Fig. 5e,f, confirming the presence of the anticipated elements in their structures. Notably, as illustrated in Fig. 5f, the EDX spectrum of TiO2/CQDs/Alg exhibits a higher carbon percentage, as well as nitrogen, compared to calcium alginate (Fig. 5e). This discrepancy can be attributed to the presence of carbon quantum dots.

Fig. 6 displays TEM images of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel. The CQDs nanoparticles are distinguishable by their dark color, measuring approximately 6 nm in size, and are dispersed on the gray surface of TiO2. Some aggregation of carbon quantum dots is evident, particularly highlighted in Fig. 6b. Additionally, a non-transparent and cloud-like layer with a size range of 12–18 nm observed on the surface of the TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite is attributed to the polymer coating, signifying the encapsulation of TiO2/CQDs by the polymer.

The investigation of porosity and surface area for both TiO2 nanoparticles and the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite is detailed through the analysis of N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, as illustrated in Fig. 7a,b. The isotherms exhibit characteristics of type IV, accompanied by hysteresis loops that are indicative of typical mesoporous features [63]. Complementing this, the BJH curves presented in Fig. S1 elucidate the pore size distribution for TiO2 and the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite within the ranges of 1.21–8 nm and 1.7–60 nm, respectively. This reaffirms the mesoporous nature of the materials [64]. Notably, the mesoporous structure of the TiO2 sample demonstrates a broad pore-size distribution, whereas the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite displays a more evenly distributed pore-size range. Consequently, the mesoporous nanocomposite hydrogel, characterized by an average pore size of approximately 3.5 nm, exhibits enhanced adsorption capacity for MB molecules, given the molecular size of MB is only 1.382 nm [65]. This enhancement augments the photocatalytic efficiency of the hydrogel nanocomposite.

Examining the BET plot results revealed that the specific surface area of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel (25 m2/g) was comparatively lower than that of pure TiO2 (71.26 m2/g). This discrepancy is attributed to the presence of alginate, which covers the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. Furthermore, the alginate coating on the TiO2 surface contributes to a reduction in the mean pore diameter of the hydrogel nanocomposite (6.85 nm) compared to that of pure TiO2 (16.49 nm). This finding is corroborated by the FE-SEM image of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite, which distinctly portrays its dense texture, along with small pores.

The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of different samples were investigated to evaluate their photo-responsive capabilities, as illustrated in Fig. 7c. Notably, TiO2/CQDs 1 %, TiO2/CQDs 5 %, and TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposites exhibited heightened absorption in both the visible and UV regions. In contrast, TiO2 nanoparticles displayed strong absorption exclusively in the UV region. The introduction of CQDs onto the TiO2 surface extended the absorption response into the visible range. This phenomenon can be attributed to the light-absorbing properties of CQDs in the visible and near-infrared regions, coupled with the quantum confinement effect. The loading of CQDs on the TiO2 surface resulted in a broadening of the energy band and an elevation of the conduction band position, facilitating the alignment of energy bands between TiO2 and CQDs. This extension of the photo-response range enhanced the photocatalyst's ability to absorb more photons, thereby improving the efficiency of visible light harvesting [66]. The absorption in the visible region exhibited an augmentation with an increase in the percentage of CQDs from 1 % to 5 %. Consequently, the TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite at 5 %, characterized by higher visible absorption, was selected as an effective nanocomposite for the preparation of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel photocatalyst [51]. Upon immobilizing TiO2/CQDs 5 % into the CaAlg matrix, the DRS spectrum revealed a further enhancement in visible light absorption intensity compared to the TiO2/CQDs 5 % nanocomposite. This enhancement can be attributed to the presence of calcium and carbon of alginate, acting as dopants for the titania matrix. Therefore, the TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel can efficiently harvest visible light to generate photoelectrons and holes [67,68].

In the investigation of the adsorption process and determination of the adsorption capacity of TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel towards MB, a series of adsorption experiments were conducted. The initial concentration of MB plays a crucial role as a driving force to overcome mass transfer resistances between the MB solution and the solid phase. Variations in the MB initial concentration, ranging from 5 to 30 ppm at neutral pH, were systematically examined. Notably, the adsorption capacity of TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel exhibited an ascending trend, increasing from 19.74 to 107.1 mg/g as the MB initial concentration rose from 5 to 30 ppm (refer to Fig. 7d). It is noteworthy that the removal efficiency displayed a distinct pattern, ascending from 59 % to 68 % as the initial MB concentration increased from 5 to 10 ppm. Subsequently, the removal efficiency experienced a decline to 56 % at 15 ppm, followed by an increase to 66 % at 30 ppm. Consequently, the optimal concentration for equilibrium studies was identified as a 10 ppm MB solution, demonstrating maximum removal efficiency. The impact of contact time on MB adsorption by TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel was investigated, as depicted in Fig. 7e. The MB removal rate exhibited a rapid increase within the initial 45 min, reaching its peak value at 90 minutes (44.13 mg/g). The attainment of equilibrium adsorption within the subsequent 45 min marked the equilibrium contact time for TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel. This dynamic equilibrium signifies the balance between the adsorbed and desorbed amounts of MB. The time required to achieve this equilibrium reflects the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent under the specified operating conditions. The notable high adsorption capacity, recorded at 44.13 mg/g in 90 min, can be attributed to the presence of alginate with carboxylate and hydroxyl functional groups in the anionic polysaccharide layer. These functional groups play a pivotal role in capturing MB molecules, acting as a cationic dye, through electrostatic attraction in aqueous solutions [69,70].

In the examination of the adsorption process mechanism, we employed the following linear formulations of the pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) models to effectively characterize the kinetics data [71].
(4) Pseudo-first-order: ln (qe – qt) = ln qe - k1 t
(5) Pseudo-second-order:t/q
t
= 1/k2qe 2 + t/q
e

The variables qe and qt represent the adsorption capacities (mg/g) at equilibrium and time (t), respectively. Additionally, the rate constants of the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, denoted as k1 (min⁻¹) and k2 (g mg⁻¹ min⁻¹), were determined through the analysis of ln (qe – qt) versus t and (t/qt) versus t plots, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. S2, the correlation coefficient (R²) value for the pseudo-second-order model was 0.9434, surpassing that of the pseudo-first-order model. Furthermore, the computed qe value for the pseudo-second order closely aligned with the experimental counterpart, as evidenced in Table 1. Consequently, it can be affirmed that the pseudo-second-order kinetic model aptly elucidates the adsorption behavior of MB on the nanocomposite hydrogel.

The investigation of adsorption isotherm models, which incorporate both equilibrium data and adsorption properties, plays a crucial role in elucidating the interaction mechanisms between pollutants and adsorbent materials [72]. Freundlich and Langmuir isotherms, commonly used in studying adsorption equilibrium, provide valuable insights among the various theories in this field. Langmuir's theoretical framework posits that the adsorption onto a solid surface follows a kinetic principle, involving a continuous bombardment of molecules onto the surface with corresponding desorption or evaporation, ensuring an equilibrium with zero accumulation rate at the surface [73]. Notably, the rates of adsorption and desorption should be equal [72]. In contrast, the Freundlich adsorption isotherm model characterizes the reversible and non-ideal adsorption process, allowing for its application to multilayer adsorption. Unlike the Langmuir model, Freundlich does not constrain itself to monolayer formation, and in this model, the distribution of adsorption heat and affinities need not be uniform across the heterogeneous surface [74].

The linear expressions for the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms are represented by the following equations [75]:
(6) Langmuir model:C

(7) Freundlich model:logq e
= log K

e

where Ce (mg/L) represents the concentration of adsorbate at equilibrium, qm (mg/g) is a constant, and KL (L/mg) as the Langmuir constant associated with adsorption capacity. Additionally, KF (L/mg) and 1/n serve as the Freundlich constants, representing adsorption capacity and intensity, respectively. Evaluation of the applicability of these models is crucial, and the curves depicting the Freundlich and Langmuir isotherms for TiO2/CQDs/Alg are illustrated in Fig. S3 (a) and (b), respectively. The effectiveness of these models was assessed through the determination of the R2 coefficient. Remarkably, the R2 value for the Freundlich model surpassed that of the Langmuir model, signifying its superior ability to fit the experimental data. Furthermore, the favorable nature of adsorption is indicated by the value of 1/n (0 < 0.6774 < 1), as evidenced in Table 1.
First of all, to achieve the improved photodegradation outcomes, the optimization of some key factors, including pollutant concentration, pH, and catalyst amount were investigated.

The MB initial concentration effect on the photodegradation efficiency of MB was studied using concentrations ranging from 5 to 30 mg/L with an 8 mg TiO2/CQDs/Alg photocatalyst dose, under visible light for 120 min. The results, depicted in Fig. S4 (a), indicate a negative correlation between the initial dye concentration and its degradation rate. This is attributed to increased adsorption of organic substances on the photocatalyst's surface, reducing photogenerated reactive radicals (O2 ˙ − and OH˙) as the main force of degradation. Moreover, elevated dye solution concentrations impede photon absorption, resulting in a decreased percentage of degradation [76].

Examining the impact of catalyst dose on photocatalytic performance is crucial, and the influence of catalyst dose on the photocatalytic degradation of MB was investigated in the catalyst concentration range of 2–10 mg. Fig. S4 (b) illustrates that as the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nano-photocatalyst amount increases, there is a corresponding improvement in MB removal efficiency due to the expansion of the nano-photocatalyst's active surface. The degradation slope of MB initially rises, reaching a peak at 8 mg, but further increasing the photocatalyst amount to 10 mg results in a decline in MB removal rate. This decrease is attributed to a reduction in the surface area of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg photocatalyst caused by an accumulation of photocatalyst. Additionally, at higher catalyst amounts (10 mg), increased turbidity hampers visible radiation penetration, leading to a reduction in pollutant degradation rates [77].

The pH of the solution plays a crucial role in influencing and regulating the surface chemistry of the adsorbent-photocatalyst, affecting the interaction of dye molecules with the adsorbent material through protonated or deprotonated surface charges at different pH values.

The pH drift method was employed to determine the point of zero charge (pHzpc). The ΔpH(pHinitial - pHfinal) was plotted against the initial pH values, with the pHzpc being the pH at which ΔpH equals zero. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the pHzpc for the TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel is 4.67. When the pH is below pHzpc, the hydrogel surface exhibits a net positive charge, whereas above pHzpc, the surface becomes negatively charged. Low adsorption is anticipated at pH levels below pHzpc, as both the hydrogel (adsorbent) and MB (adsorbate) are positively charged, leading to dominant electrostatic repulsion.

In the assessment of MB degradation efficiency, the pH of solutions was adjusted within the range of 2–10 by adding KOH (0.1 M) or HCl (0.1 M) before introducing the photocatalyst. As depicted in Fig. S4 (c), the results reveal a direct correlation between pH increase and the rate of photocatalytic degradation by the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel. A decrease in pH introduces a more positive charge to the Alg layer, resulting from the introduction of H+ ions that protonate carboxylic groups within the hydrogel structure. Under acidic conditions (pH < pKa), the dominant species of MB (pKa = 5.6) is the ionic form MB+, primarily due to the positive charge of the amino group and sulfur. This leads to electrostatic repulsion between MB+ and the positive charge of the Alg layer, causing a decrease in MB photodegradation. Conversely, under basic conditions, the non-ionic form of MB prevails, causing biopolymer carboxylic groups to switch to negatively charged carboxylate ions (COO-) [47,78]. This enhances the interaction of MB molecules with the negative surface charge of Alg, resulting in increased MB adsorption and, consequently, higher removal of these molecules. This result is consistent with the findings from the previously mentioned pHzpc study. Additionally, in more basic conditions, an increase in hydroxyl ions in the reaction medium induces the formation of more hydroxyl radicals through the photocatalytic process, leading to further degradation of MB [6].

Consequently, the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel exhibited noteworthy photocatalytic efficacy, achieving substantial MB degradation with 5 ppm of MB and 400 ppm of the photocatalyst at pH = 10 within 120 min.

The degradation of MB through photocatalysis was investigated using TiO2 nanoparticles, TiO2/CQDs 5 % nanocomposite, and TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel under visible light. Fig. 7f shows the photodegradation efficiency of MB for synthesized photocatalysts in different irradiation times.

In the absence of light, the hydrogel demonstrated the adsorption capacity, trapping 22 % of MB during a 15-min adsorption-desorption equilibrium, whereas TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2/CQDs 5 % nanocomposite showed no adsorption ability in darkness. This behavior is attributed to active sites and the Alg layer's electrostatic attraction, enabling effective trapping of MB molecules by the hydrogel.

The lower degradation efficiency of TiO2 nanoparticles (18 %) compared to TiO2/CQDs 5 % (41 %) is attributed to the large band gap (Eg = 3.2 eV) and the UV-light response of TiO2 nanoparticles. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of TiO2/CQDs can be explained in two ways. First, CQDs facilitate the transfer of photoelectrons from the TiO2 surface and act as an electron sink, suppressing the recombination of electron-hole pairs. [79,80]. Secondly, the upconversion photoluminescence of CQDs increases solar light utilization by absorbing visible light and emitting light in the 325–425 nm range [81], exciting adjacent TiO2 nanoparticles and generating electron-hole pairs. These pairs react with O2 and OH−, converting them into superoxide radical anions (O2˙−) and OH˙ radicals, leading to MB degradation. Furthermore, the photodegradation efficiency of TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel is an outstanding 97.12 %, attributed to the synergistic effect of adsorption on the Alg surface, facilitating electron immigration [82]. Studies affirm that the shape of photocatalysts influences the photodegradation process, with photocatalysts embedded in biopolymers exhibiting superior performance [83,84]. Additionally, the higher visible light absorption intensity of the hydrogel, as revealed by DRS analysis, compared to TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs 5 %, contributes to the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency of the hydrogel under visible light.

The kinetic curves for MB photodegradation over the TiO2, TiO2/CQDs 5 %, and TiO2/CQDs/Alg photocatalysts indicated that the photodecomposition of MB follows the pseudo-first-order kinetics as per the Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) mechanism (Fig. 7g). According to IUPAC guidelines [74], the L-H mechanism involves surface catalysis where the reaction takes place between adsorbed species. The L-H model can be expressed as:
(8)

Here, C0 represents the initial concentration of MB (mg/L), Ct is the concentration of MB (mg/L) after visible light exposure at time t, and k (min−1) is the kinetic rate constant. Upon plotting the Ln
(

versus illumination time ( t ), the k values for TiO2, TiO2/CQDs 5 %, and TiO2/CQDs/Alg photocatalysts were 0.0016 min−1, 0.004 min−1, and 0.0229 min−1, respectively. Consequently, the TiO2/CQDs/Alg photocatalyst demonstrated the highest MB photodegradation rate. It is assumed that the photocatalytic degradation occurs concurrently with the adsorption process, suggesting that the degradation reaction primarily takes place between the surface-adsorbed dyes and the photogenerated oxidants, although other pathways may also exist [85].

Active species, such as h+, OH˙, and O2˙−, assume pivotal roles in the photocatalytic redox reactions. This study seeks a nuanced understanding of their involvement in the photodegradation of MB, employing diverse charge carrier scavengers, including methanol (OH˙ capture agent), EDTA (h+ capture agent), and BQ (O2˙− capture agent). These investigations, meticulously conducted under conditions akin to photocatalytic efficiency experiments with scavenger concentrations at 1 mM [46]. Notably, the introduction of BQ into the system resulted in a substantial reduction (37 %) in the photocatalytic degradation of MB by the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel, as depicted in Fig. 7h. Conversely, the addition of methanol and EDTA yielded no discernible impact on the degradation rate of MB. Therefore, it can be inferred that O2˙− radicals prominently serve as the primary reactive species, while holes and OH˙ radicals assume subordinate roles in the degradation mechanism.

Considering the experimental results and analyses mentioned earlier, the remarkable photocatalytic efficacy of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel can be ascribed to several key factors. First, the strong visible light response exhibited by TiO2/CQDs played a pivotal role. Second, the highly dispersed TiO2/CQDs nanocomposites, achieved through immobilization and encapsulation into the Alg support, fostered the creation of a porous structure. Third, the TiO2/CQDs/Alg configuration was rich in active sites, facilitating the adsorption of MB and promoting photocatalytic reactions. Consequently, a proposed mechanism delineating the degradation of MB by the TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel photocatalyst is elucidated in Fig. 8.

Upon irradiation with visible light, the doped CQDs on the TiO2 surfaces undergo an upconversion process, leading to the excitation of electrons from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of TiO2, generating electron/hole (e­/h+) pairs [45]. CQDs, functioning as electron sinks, efficiently facilitate the transfer of photo-generated electrons from the TiO2 surface, thereby preventing recombination with holes. The photo-generated electrons on CQDs can react with dissolved O2, producing O2˙− radicals with the Alg layer's assistance. Simultaneously, A significant number of photo-generated holes remain in the VB of TiO2, transferring to the Alg layer and effecting the reduction of H2O into the reactive species OH˙ radical. Subsequent to these processes, the highly oxidizing reagents (O2˙− and OH˙) engage in further reactions with adsorbed MB on the Alg surface, resulting in its complete degradation and mineralization. Additionally, the Alg support acts as both an electron donor and a favorable environment for the reverse migration of e­/h+ pairs [33,66]. The photo-generated holes, possessing robust oxidative properties, directly participate in the degradation of MB. In summation, the TiO2/CQDs/Alg configuration, characterized by its abundance of surface-active sites, manifests outstanding photocatalytic activity.( Fig. 8)

The ability of hydrogels to swell is greatly influenced by minor variations in the surrounding liquid. Specifically, the pH levels have a significant impact on the swelling of hydrogels. Various experiments were conducted to examine the swelling behavior across a pH range from 2 to 12. The results of these tests are illustrated in Fig. S6, depicting the swelling ratio in relation to the pH of the solution. The TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel exhibits significant swelling behavior across the pH range of 2–12, with swelling ratios ranging from 31 % to 137 %. This behavior underscores the hydrogel's remarkable stability over a wide range of pH. The presence of TiO2 and CQDs nanoparticles with hydrophilic functional groups in the alginate matrix likely serves as cross-linking agents. Prior research suggests that incorporating TiO2 and carbonaceous materials with active functional groups (such as OH, NH2, COOH, …) into the alginate matrix can produce strong interfacial interactions, thereby enhancing swelling, mechanical strength, and thermal stability. [86–89]. Moreover, the hydrophilic TiO2 nanoparticles, characterized by a high surface area, along with the intermolecular spaces within the 3D hydrogel network, enable enhanced water absorption, making it suitable for wastewater treatment [90].

The stability and the reusability of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel photocatalyst are crucial indicators of its cost-effectiveness and usefulness. Therefore, Fig. 9 shows the results of five consecutive recycling experiments for the photodegradation of MB under visible light. The graph illustrates that the effectiveness of the TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel photocatalyst slightly decreased but showed minimal deactivation over the five cycles. This confirms the strong stability of the hydrogel photocatalyst being studied.

In this study, the TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel was synthesized by a facile synthesis route of combining TiO2, CQDs, and CaAlg polymer and introduced as an integrated photocatalyst/adsorbent system for removal of MB pollutant. The doping of CQDs on the surface of TiO2 improved the separation of electron-hole pairs by electron transportation from TiO2 surface and expanded the light absorption to the visible region, confirming with DRS analysis. Alginate biopolymer was used as a robust solid support for immobilizing the TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite and provided a porous hydrogel structure for photocatalyst. Characterization outcomes affirmed the successful dispersion and stabilization of the TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite within the CaAlg matrix. The TiO2/CQDs/Alg nanocomposite hydrogel exhibited a notable adsorption capacity (44.13 mg/g at 90 min) for MB, attributed to the active sites on the Alg surface. Remarkably, this hydrogel displayed high photocatalytic activity (97 %) in the visible light-induced degradation of MB, surpassing the performance of TiO2 nanoparticles and TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite. The observed efficacy could be attributed to a synergistic interplay of adsorption and photocatalysis. The TiO2/CQDs/Alg hydrogel demonstrated impressive reusability in the degradation of MB for five consecutive cycles and displayed notable swelling across a wide pH range, highlighting the hydrogel's remarkable stability. Furthermore, experiments with charge carrier scavengers exhibited that O2˙− radicals predominantly drove the photocatalytic degradation process. These findings present a promising solution for environmentally friendly and sustainable wastewater remediation applications.
